phases.post_rigorous: why a random point almost never lies

stuck_points_predicted: thinks a large field is needed for completeness
stuck_points_addressed: thinks a large field is needed for completeness

claims: two degree-2 polynomials over GF(101) differing only in the linear term agree at exactly one point

comprehension_set.question: State the Schwartz–Zippel bound for a degree-d polynomial over a field F.
comprehension_set.answer_key: A nonzero degree-d polynomial has at most d roots, so a random point is a root with probability <= d/|F|.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: thinks a large field is needed for completeness

comprehension_set.question: Two degree-2 polynomials over GF(101) differ only in the linear term. At how many points do they agree?
comprehension_set.answer_key: Their difference is a nonzero degree-1 polynomial, so they agree at exactly one point.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: thinks a large field is needed for completeness

comprehension_set.question: Why does a larger field make polynomial identity testing more reliable?
comprehension_set.answer_key: The error probability d/|F| shrinks as |F| grows, so a single random check is more decisive.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: thinks a large field is needed for completeness

comprehension_set.question: Re-derive the bound: if p != q have degree <= d, why can they agree at most d times?
comprehension_set.answer_key: p - q is a nonzero polynomial of degree <= d, and a nonzero degree-d polynomial has at most d roots.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: thinks a large field is needed for completeness
